old_claim.note: Advanced Hunting & Gathering provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Glossed flint blades from Ohalo II provide source-backed harvesting-tool evidence for planned wild-cereal harvesting in the cultivation context.

source_shape.source_locator: The use-wear analysis reports glossed flint blades at Ohalo II used for harvesting near-ripe semi-green wild cereals and describes them as the earliest composite cereal-harvesting tools.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source directly documents stone harvesting tools in the same Ohalo II wild-cereal cultivation context.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The corrected prerequisite identifies the concrete tool capability visible in the evidence, while the former advanced_hunting_gathering edge was a later-dated social context and not a source-backed cultivation mechanism.

ontology_before: Horticulture depended on advanced_hunting_gathering, a broad and later-dated social-context node that created a chronology conflict after anchoring horticulture to Ohalo II.
ontology_after: Horticulture depends on advanced_stone_tools as the source-backed harvesting-tool capability documented in the Ohalo II cultivation evidence.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: horticulture now depends on advanced_stone_tools and no longer directly depends on advanced_hunting_gathering.
would_reject_if: The horticulture node were later rescoped back to a generic post-10000 BCE social transition rather than the Ohalo II cultivation evidence.
A source showed Ohalo II cultivation did not involve flint harvesting tools or planned cereal harvesting.
The advanced_hunting_gathering node were source-checked and redated before Ohalo II with a specific plant-cultivation mechanism.
short_reason: The Ohalo II source supports a stone harvesting-tool edge, not a later-dated advanced hunter-gatherer prerequisite.
